clean_story:
Stone throwers. Castle breakers.
The term siege engine comes from the Latin obsidio (to besiege)—machines built to starve, smash, or scale walls when brute force failed.

A trebuchet isn’t just a giant slingshot. It’s a 30-foot-tall lever, using a 20-ton counterweight to fling 300-pound boulders 300 yards—like a baseball pitcher, but with a car. The Warwolf, built for Edward I in 1304, hurled stones so hard it collapsed Stirling Castle’s curtain wall in one shot. Yet precision mattered: Engineers adjusted sling length and weight like tuning a guitar, turning guesswork into repeatable destruction.

Battering rams were mobile log cabins on wheels, their iron-tipped beams swung by 50 men inside—a human-powered jackhammer against oak gates. The Helepolis ("Taker of Cities"), a 130-foot wooden tower rolled to walls, let soldiers drop bridges onto battlements like a firetruck ladder.

Three related subjects are catapult physics, castle murder holes, and gunpowder’s siege takeover.
